narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 92% chance you'll struggle to pass the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—along with a relatively low undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and an LSAT score of 22.70, both well below averages around 3.21 and 36.70. You're from a middle-income family, identify as a Native American male planning full-time studies, which aligns with many applicants but stands out in some ways, like your racial background being less common in the dataset at just 1.8% compared to over 80% White students.

The model's decision was driven most strongly by your third-year school decile, which had the biggest pull toward predicting bar failure, followed closely by your LSAT score. Your first-year decile came next in influence, then family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender in descending order of impact—all nudging the prediction toward failure rather than success. None of these stood out as protective factors pushing you toward passing; instead, your lower academic markers compared to peers painted a riskier picture for bar performance.

For context, typical admitted students have stronger early and later school rankings, higher test scores, and GPAs that signal consistent academic strength, helping them beat the odds on the bar. Your situation differs because these core academic signals are at the bottom end, which the model sees as the strongest warning signs from past data.

Looking ahead, you can realistically boost your chances by targeting changeable factors like retaking the LSAT to aim closer to average scores, as it's a key influencer and often improvable with focused prep. Building a stronger GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program could help offset your current deciles, showing upward trends that models value. While family background and demographics are harder to shift, excelling in full-time law studies with targeted bar prep courses has turned around similar profiles before—consider seeking mentorship from Native American lawyers or free LSAT resources to get started. With determination, revising these academic pillars could flip your prediction in future applications.
